content.text: A hook handler is any executable that reads a JSON payload from standard input, applies logic, and communicates a decision back to Claude Code through its exit code and an optional JSON object on standard output. Every hook type — shell command, HTTP endpoint, MCP tool, LLM prompt, or subagent — follows this same contract, so the skills used to write one type transfer directly to the others.
Claude Code's hook system exposes lifecycle events —

, and more — at which you can inject arbitrary behavior. Writing a hook means authoring the handler that runs when one of those events fires, then registering it in a Claude Code settings file so the runtime knows when to call it.
The simplest hook handler is a shell script. More sophisticated handlers can be HTTP servers, Python programs, or Node.js scripts. For policy decisions that require language-model reasoning, the
handler types offload the decision entirely to a Claude model without you writing any process code.

Hook handlers are evaluated at the time the event fires. Claude Code builds a JSON payload describing the event context, pipes it to the handler's stdin, waits for the handler to exit, then reads the handler's stdout to determine what action to take next.

Without hooks, Claude Code's behavior is entirely shaped by CLAUDE.md instructions and the permissions configuration. Both of those influence Claude's reasoning, but neither can interrupt an action mid-execution, observe what actually happened after a tool ran, or react in real time to events outside the agentic loop. Hooks fill that gap.
Consider three common pain points hooks address directly. First, a developer wants every file write to be auto-formatted but cannot guarantee Claude always adds the formatter call to its plan. A
hook on Edit/Write unconditionally runs prettier after each write regardless of what Claude intended. Second, a team wants to prevent Claude from ever running
hook with exit code 2 blocks those calls at the system level before Claude even tries. Third, a CI pipeline needs to notify Claude when a background test suite fails. An
hook runs the suite in the background and wakes Claude with the failure output only if something breaks.

These patterns are impossible to express purely through instructions or permissions. Hooks turn Claude Code from a self-contained assistant into an extensible platform that can integrate with any shell tooling, external service, or LLM-powered policy engine.

Hooks run unconditionally when an event fires, regardless of what Claude planned. A formatter hook always formats; a logger hook always logs — without relying on Claude remembering to add those steps.
hook is a kernel-level block — the tool call never executes, even if Claude's reasoning concluded it should.
hook can prepend environment metadata — git branch, current directory, build status — to every prompt without modifying CLAUDE.md.
, a hook can run a long-lived test suite or linter in the background and only interrupt Claude if the result is negative, with zero blocking latency.
Any executable works — bash, Python, Go binary, Node.js script. The only requirement is that it reads stdin and writes to stdout/stderr.
Because hooks are shell processes, they compose naturally with grep, jq, curl, git, and any CLI tool in the environment.
add at minimum one process spawn per event. A naive hook that calls a slow external API in-process can noticeably stall every tool call.
Hook scripts live outside the normal codebase review path. A hook that blocks
in 2024 may cause confusing silent failures if the project directory structure changes in 2026.
A hook reads event payloads that may include content from tool outputs or external sources. A hook that pipes untrusted content directly into a shell command is vulnerable to injection. The
filter fails open, so it cannot be relied upon as a security boundary.
fails silently if those binaries are not in PATH. Hooks committed to a shared repo must document their runtime requirements.
A hook that stalls past its timeout is terminated with a soft error. The default for
hooks is only 30 seconds; slow operations must be made async.
When an event fires, Claude Code serialises the event context to a JSON object and writes it to the handler's standard input. All payloads share a common envelope:
. The payload is always valid JSON, always UTF-8, always on a single line with no trailing newline.
The exit code is the primary decision signal and is the only thing Claude Code requires from a handler. If the handler exits before writing any stdout, the exit code alone determines the outcome:
Logging, formatting, context injection with a JSON body
Hook pre-check failed non-critically; telemetry hooks that fail should not block
PreToolUse: deny tool call. asyncRewake: pass stderr to Claude as a system reminder.

The exit code alone is sufficient for blocking or allowing. To supply a richer response — a denial reason, additional context, or a directive to continue the conversation — write a JSON object to stdout. The object must have a
key whose value is an event-specific inner object:
, a hook can inject additional context into Claude's prompt by setting
causes Claude Code to restart the turn, allowing a hook to trigger a follow-up action after Claude finishes. If stdout is empty or contains invalid JSON, Claude Code ignores it gracefully.

The diagram below shows the complete I/O pipeline for a command-type hook handler. Settings registration, event dispatch, stdin delivery, script logic, and result processing all form a linear sequence. HTTP and MCP tool handlers follow the same flow but use a POST request or tool call instead of a process spawn.

attrs.alt: Hook Script I/O Pipeline: settings.json registers the handler, Claude Code fires an event and pipes JSON to stdin, the script processes it and returns an exit code plus optional JSON stdout, Claude Code acts on the result.

content.text: Key points from the diagram: the payload always flows in via stdin; the script never needs to call Claude Code back directly; and the only output channels are stdout (JSON decision) and the exit code. Stderr is captured by Claude Code but only shown to the user when
When to Use Hooks (and When Not To)
Hooks are the right tool when:
The behavior must happen unconditionally — not subject to Claude's planning.
The logic depends on runtime data (what command is actually in the payload, what the file path is) rather than on intent.
A background process needs to feed results back to Claude without blocking the main session.
An external service (CI, Slack, telemetry endpoint) must be notified of every tool use.
The goal is to guide Claude's behavior through instructions — CLAUDE.md and the system prompt are better suited.
The requirement is to allow or deny specific tool names statically — the
section of settings.json handles this with zero process overhead.
The action is a one-time setup. Use
hooks sparingly; they run on every session start including quick throwaway sessions.
inside the project. The minimal structure for a Bash command hook is:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# .claude/hooks/pre-tool.sh
set -euo pipefail

# Read the full JSON payload from stdin
input=$(cat)

# Extract fields with jq (must be installed)
hook_event=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.hook_event_name')
tool_name=$(echo "$input"  | jq -r '.tool_name // empty')

# Example: block any Bash command containing 'rm -rf /'
if [[ "$tool_name" == "Bash" ]]; then
  cmd=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.tool_input.command // empty')
  if echo "$cmd" | grep -qE 'rm -rf /'; then
    echo '{"hookSpecificOutput":{"hookEventName":"PreToolUse","permissionDecision":"deny","permissionDecisionReason":"Blanket rm -rf / is blocked"}}'
    exit 2
  fi
fi

# Default: allow
exit 0
Add the hook entry under the event name in
. Each event key maps to an array of hook groups; each group has a
environment variable is set by Claude Code to the absolute path of the project root, making the path portable across machines. Use it instead of a hard-coded absolute path.
Before Claude Code ever fires the hook, test it by piping a synthetic payload directly:

# Test: should exit 2 and print the deny JSON
echo '{"hook_event_name":"PreToolUse","session_id":"test","tool_name":"Bash","tool_input":{"command":"rm -rf /var"}}' \
  | .claude/hooks/pre-tool.sh
echo "Exit code: $?"

# Test: should exit 0 silently
echo '{"hook_event_name":"PreToolUse","session_id":"test","tool_name":"Bash","tool_input":{"command":"ls -la"}}' \
  | .claude/hooks/pre-tool.sh
echo "Exit code: $?"
Example 1 — Auto-format after every file write (PostToolUse)

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# .claude/hooks/format.sh
# Runs prettier on every file Claude writes or edits.
set -euo pipefail

input=$(cat)
tool=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.tool_name')

# PostToolUse for Edit/Write passes file_path in tool_input
file=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.tool_input.file_path // empty')

if [[ -n "$file" && -f "$file" ]]; then
  # Suppress non-zero exit so hook exit code stays 0
  npx prettier --write "$file" 2>/dev/null || true
fi

exit 0
Example 2 — Inject git context on every prompt (UserPromptSubmit)

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# .claude/hooks/git-context.sh
# Prepends the current branch, last commit, and dirty status to every prompt.
set -uo pipefail

branch=$(git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo 'unknown')
last=$(git log -1 --oneline 2>/dev/null || echo 'no commits')
status=$(git status --short 2>/dev/null | head -5 || echo '')

context="[git branch: $branch | last commit: $last]"
[[ -n "$status" ]] && context="$context\n[unstaged changes:\n$status]"

# Emit the additionalContext key so Claude Code appends it to the prompt
printf '{"hookSpecificOutput":{"hookEventName":"UserPromptSubmit","additionalContext":"%s"}}' \
  "$(echo -e "$context" | sed 's/"/\\"/g')"

exit 0
Example 4 — Background CI watcher with asyncRewake

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# .claude/hooks/run-tests.sh
# Runs affected tests in the background. Wakes Claude only on failure.
set -uo pipefail

file=$(cat | jq -r '.tool_input.file_path // empty')

# Determine affected test file
test_file=$(echo "$file" | sed 's|src/||; s|\.ts$|.test.ts|')

if [[ -f "$test_file" ]]; then
  # Run tests; capture output
  output=$(npx jest "$test_file" --no-coverage 2>&1) || {
    # Exit 2 wakes Claude via asyncRewake; stderr goes to Claude as a reminder
    echo "Tests failed for $test_file:" >&2
    echo "$output" >&2
    exit 2
  }
fi

exit 0
A fintech team uses Claude Code for automated PR generation. They maintain three hooks that together enforce their development policy without requiring any CLAUDE.md instructions about it:
— A Python hook parses the shell command with
that targets a branch whose name does not start with
. This prevents accidental pushes to protected branches even in
on every TypeScript file Claude touches. Claude never needs instructions about formatting.

on affected test files. If any test fails, Claude is woken with the full vitest output as a system reminder. Claude then reads the output, diagnoses the failure, and edits the code. This creates a tight edit-test-fix loop without any human involvement.

The hooks add roughly 200ms of latency per tool call (mostly process spawn overhead), which is acceptable given that the alternative is human review of every Claude action. The team stores all hooks in
) so every team member inherits them automatically on clone.
, a hook can silently succeed (exit 0) even when
returns a non-zero exit because the field doesn't exist, leading to false-negative blocks or missed allowances.

# Wrong: $file is unquoted and jq failure is swallowed
file=$(jq -r .tool_input.file_path)
npx prettier --write $file

# Correct:
set -euo pipefail
file=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.tool_input.file_path // empty')
[[ -n "$file" ]] && npx prettier --write "$file"
Confusing exit code 1 and exit code 2
to block a tool call. Exit 1 is a soft error — Claude Code logs it and continues. Only
blocks execution. When testing whether blocking works, always verify the exit code, not just the JSON output.
Writing the JSON to stderr instead of stdout

. Writing the denial JSON to stderr results in the block being silently ignored while the tool call proceeds. Debug output (progress, intermediate values) belongs on stderr; the decision JSON must go to stdout.

hook fires on the UI critical path — the user submitted a message and is waiting for a response. A hook that calls a slow external API synchronously here will make the UI appear to hang. Either cache results aggressively or move slow work to an

# DANGEROUS: passes tool_input.command directly into eval
cmd=$(cat | jq -r '.tool_input.command')
eval "$cmd"  # an attacker-controlled command runs with hook privileges

# Safe: read the payload but never eval untrusted content
input=$(cat)
cmd=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.tool_input.command // empty')
# Inspect $cmd with string comparisons / grep, never eval it
A hook that formats files, runs tests, and logs to a remote API is hard to debug and hard to disable selectively. Split concerns into separate scripts so each can be toggled independently.
Set explicit timeouts for slow hooks.
If the hook can be slow, add a
field to the handler object and use
for anything that could take more than a few seconds.
Use the if field to avoid unnecessary spawns.
hook on Bash that only cares about
so it is not spawned for every ls or cat command.
Test hooks offline before using them in a live session.
Pipe synthetic payloads to the script directly from the terminal. Cover the allow path and the block path.
, or a specific npm package, note that in the project README or CLAUDE.md so teammates know what to install.
Prefer project-level hooks for team policy.
are committed to the repo and apply to every team member. Hooks in
Never trust payload content as safe for shell expansion.
Treat every field in the JSON payload as untrusted user input. Use string comparison, regex matching through grep/Python, or a proper parser — never
on anything extracted from the payload.
Process spawn cost dominates hook latency for short-lived scripts. On a modern system, spawning a bash process, sourcing
takes roughly 30–80ms. For a session that issues 100 tool calls, a synchronous PreToolUse hook adds 3–8 seconds of wall-clock latency. Mitigations:
to avoid spawning for irrelevant tool calls. A hook that applies only to
Prefer compiled binaries or Python/Node for complex hooks.
A Go or Python binary avoids bash profile sourcing and generally starts faster.
Anything that hits the network or runs a test suite should use
. This detaches the slow work from the hot path entirely.
A hook that validates against a remote policy API can write the result to a local file with a TTL and skip the network call for subsequent events within the cache window.
have a hard ceiling of 30 seconds before Claude Code terminates them. Handlers on
have a ceiling of 10 seconds. These events are on UI-critical paths. Tool-event handlers default to 600 seconds but should still be async-ified if they are slow.
The standard CLI for reading hook payloads. Nearly every bash hook starts with
Claude Code sets several environment variables that hooks can read:
Absolute path to the project root (where .claude/ lives)
Space-separated list of file paths affected by a write/edit batch
Current session UUID (also in the stdin payload)
For logic that is too complex to express in a shell script, the
handler type sends the event payload to a Claude model with a yes/no question. The
handler type (experimental) spawns a full subagent that can use Read, Grep, and Glob to verify conditions before returning:
No — Claude decides whether to call
Only what Claude passes as args
The if field is not a security boundary
condition on a hook handler uses permission-rule syntax for sub-command filtering. It is a
, not a security control. When a Bash command can't be parsed (complex quoting, heredocs, process substitution), the
filter fails open — the hook runs. Rely on the hook's own internal logic and exit codes for actual enforcement.

The stdin payload can contain content from external sources — command arguments, file content, API responses that Claude fetched. A hook that extracts a value from the payload and interpolates it unsafely into a shell command is vulnerable to injection. The critical rule:
never eval or shell-expand values extracted from the payload

# Safe: compare the extracted value as a string
cmd=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.tool_input.command // empty')
if [[ "$cmd" == *'rm -rf /'* ]]; then
  exit 2
fi

# Safe: pass to grep as a fixed-string match
if echo "$cmd" | grep -qF 'rm -rf /'; then
  exit 2
fi

A hook that crashes (unhandled exception, missing dependency, permission error) exits with a non-zero code. If that code is 1, Claude Code logs it and continues — the hook's intention is silently lost. If the hook is critical (must block), make sure it emits exit 2 explicitly rather than relying on the shell's default exit-on-error behavior, which might produce exit 1 instead of exit 2. Add a trap to catch unexpected failures:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -uo pipefail

# If anything below throws unexpectedly, fail safe by blocking
trap 'echo "Unexpected hook failure" >&2; exit 2' ERR

input=$(cat)
# ... your logic ...
exit 0
Think of a hook handler as

. A web framework's middleware intercepts every HTTP request, inspects it, optionally modifies the request or response, and either passes it through or short-circuits with an error. A Claude Code hook intercepts every lifecycle event in the exact same way — it receives the event context, inspects it, optionally enriches the session context (via
), and either passes through (exit 0) or blocks (exit 2).

The parallel extends further: just as middleware should be fast, stateless, and idempotent to avoid making the application brittle, hook handlers should be fast (or async), stateless (no side-effects on the Claude Code session other than the one they're designed for), and idempotent (running the same hook twice on the same event should produce the same result). A hook that writes to a shared file or database without locking is the equivalent of a middleware that has a race condition.
The key difference from web middleware: Claude Code hooks communicate
through exit codes and stdout. There is no bidirectional socket, no shared memory, and no callback API. The handler's only power over Claude Code is what it writes to stdout before exiting.
A hook handler reads the event payload from
as JSON, applies logic, and communicates its decision via exit code and optional
JSON on stdout — those are the only two output channels.
Exit code 0 means success / allow; exit code 1 means soft error and continues;
exit code 2 is the only way to hard-block
a tool call or rewake Claude from an async hook.
Test every hook offline by piping synthetic JSON payloads directly to the script before using it in a live Claude Code session.
for background test runners and CI watchers — the hook detaches from the hot path and only interrupts Claude when it exits 2.
field is a spawn-reduction optimization that fails open — never use it as a security enforcement point.
content extracted from the payload. Treat it as untrusted input and match with string comparison or grep.
Hooks are middleware — they should be fast, single-purpose, and idempotent. Move slow operations async; split multi-purpose hooks into focused single-responsibility scripts.
